---
title: "Write-Up"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    fig-cap-location: bottom
    embed-resources: true
  pdf:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    fig-cap-location: bottom
    geometry: margin=1in
---
```{python}
#| echo: false
import geopandas as gpd
import pandas as pd
import os
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from shapely import wkt
import contextily as cx
import csv
import numpy as np
from geopy.geocoders import ArcGIS
import time
from shapely.geometry import Point
import requests
from datetime import datetime
import plotly.graph_objects as go
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
DATA = os.path.join(os.getcwd(), "data", "raw-data", "Final_data")
demo = pd.read_csv(os.path.join(DATA, "cook_county_high_school_demographics.csv"))
transport = pd.read_csv(os.path.join(DATA,'CTA_-_Bus_Routes_20260129.csv'))
academic = pd.read_csv(os.path.join(DATA,"panel_yx_highschools_base_new.csv"))
cook = gpd.read_file(os.path.join(DATA,'cook_county/PVS_25_v2_tracts2020_17031.shp'))
income = pd.read_csv(os.path.join(DATA,'cook_tract_income_v7_2016_2024.csv'))
desert = pd.read_csv(os.path.join(DATA,'cook_food_access_2019.csv'))
seg = pd.read_csv(os.path.join(DATA,'school_segregation_all_final.csv'))

school_loc = gpd.read_file(os.path.join(DATA,'Public_School_Locations_2021-22.geojson'))
df = pd.read_csv(os.path.join(os.getcwd(), 'data', 'derived-data', 'final_merged.csv'))
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
cook_3857 = cook.to_crs(epsg=3857)
cook_3857.head()
threshold = 0.5
cook_clean = cook_3857.loc[:, cook_3857.apply(lambda col: ((col.isna()) | (col.isnull())).mean() <= threshold)]
cook_clean = cook_clean[['TRACTID', 'TRACTLABEL', 'POP20', 'geometry']]
cook_clean['TRACTID'] = cook_clean['TRACTID'].astype(int)

income['year'].unique()
income['median_hh_income'] = income['median_hh_income'].apply(
    lambda x: np.nan if x is not None and x < 0 else x
)
income['GEOID'] = income['GEOID'].astype(int)
cook_income = pd.merge(cook_clean, income, left_on='TRACTID', right_on='GEOID', how='right')
cook_income = cook_income[cook_income['TRACTID'].notna()]
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
school_loc = school_loc[school_loc['STATE']=='IL']
```
```{python}
#| echo: false
demo = demo.replace("*", np.nan)
demographic = demo.loc[:, demo.isna().mean() <= 0.5]
demographic = demographic[demographic['County']=='Cook']
demographic = demographic[['RCDTS', 'School Name']]

academic.columns
academic_seg = pd.merge(academic, seg, left_on=['school_name', 'year'], right_on=['school', 'year'], how='left')

demo_aca = pd.merge(demographic, academic_seg, left_on = 'RCDTS', right_on ='school_id', how='outer')
school_loc_unique = school_loc.drop_duplicates(subset='NAME')

merge_schools = pd.merge(demo_aca, school_loc_unique, left_on='School Name', right_on='NAME', how='left')
school_loc_unique.head()
##########################################################################
missing_schools = [
    {'School Name': 'Acero Chtr Sch Network -  Major Hector P Garcia MD H S', 'LAT': 41.8085443, 'LON': -87.7333591},
    {'School Name': 'Acero Chtr Sch Network- Sor Juana Ines de la Cruz K-12', 'LAT': 42.0160989, 'LON': -87.6871158},
    {'School Name': 'Collins Academy STEAM High School', 'LAT': 41.8640799, 'LON': -87.7036933}
]

for school in missing_schools:
    mask = merge_schools["School Name"] == school["School Name"]
    merge_schools.loc[mask, "LAT"] = school["LAT"]
    merge_schools.loc[mask, "LON"] = school["LON"]

###########################################################################

merge_schools_geo = gpd.GeoDataFrame(
    merge_schools,
    geometry=[Point(xy) for xy in zip(merge_schools['LON'], merge_schools['LAT'])],
    crs="EPSG:4326"  # WGS84 lat/lon
)

merge_schools_geo = merge_schools_geo.to_crs(epsg=3857)
merge_schools_geo = merge_schools_geo.to_crs(cook_clean.crs)

```

```{python}
#| echo: false
transport['geometry'] = transport['the_geom'].apply(wkt.loads)

gdf_transport = gpd.GeoDataFrame(
    transport,
    geometry="geometry",
    crs="EPSG:4326"
)
gdf_transport_3857 = gdf_transport.to_crs(epsg=3857)
```

# Disclaimer
The Coffee Coders are made up of two groups from the University of Chicago Harris School of Public Policy's Data Visualization Course, groups 24 and 49.
Coffee Coders Group 24:Dominick Sanakiewicz (dominicksanakiewicz) & Abraham Sadat (abrahamsadat96) 
Coffee Coders Group 49: Amanda Gu (AmandaAtHarris) & Zhen Zang (UChiZhen)

As discussed with Professor Peter Ganon groups #24 and #49 will have similar motivating questions, data, and methodologies. The groups also presented together. Therefore the main distinction between the two groups’ write ups will be the findings. Group #24 focused on ELA scores and chronic absenteeism, while Group #49 focused on math proficiency and 4 year graduation rates as their dependent variables.

# Motivating question
Our project aimed to explore what publicly available data can best explain the variance in education outcomes in Cook County? Cook County presents an interesting case study since it captures a vast range of incomes, education outcomes, and food security statuses. 

Figure one shows a map of Cook County. The gradient denotes the variety in incomes, with the darker colors signifying wealthier census tracts. The red dots are all of the public high schools in Chicago. Public transit is represented by the green lines on the map. We can see that the wealthiest census tracts are concentrated in both the north sides of Chicago, and Cook County. Naturally, the concentration of high schools is denser in the city of Chicago since the majority of Cook County’s residents live there. 

```{python}
#| echo: false
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 6), dpi=300)

cook_income.plot(
    column='median_hh_income',
    scheme='Quantiles',
    k=5,
    cmap='Blues',                    
    ax=ax,
    edgecolor='black',                 
    linewidth=0.5
)
merge_schools_geo.plot(
    ax=ax,
    color="red",
    markersize=20,
    alpha=0.7,
    label="schools"
)
gdf_transport_3857.plot(
    ax=ax,
    linewidth=1,
    edgecolor="green",
    alpha=0.5,
    label="Transport"
)
ax.set(title='Median Income')
ax.legend(
          fontsize=10,
          frameon=False,
          title="Map Layers",
          loc='lower left')
cx.add_basemap(
    ax,
    source=cx.providers.CartoDB.Positron
)

ax.set_axis_off()
plt.show()
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
df["income_quintile"] = pd.qcut(
    df['x_median_hh_income'].rank(method="first"),   # rank first to handle ties
    5,
    labels=["Q1 (Lowest)", "Q2", "Q3", "Q4", "Q5 (Highest)"],
)
QUINTILE_COLORS = {
    "Q1 (Lowest)":  "#d73027",   # red
    "Q2":           "#fc8d59",   # orange
    "Q3":           "#fee08b",   # yellow
    "Q4":           "#91bfdb",   # light blue
    "Q5 (Highest)": "#4575b4",   # dark blue
}
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

def plot_scatter_with_ols(
    df,
    x_col,
    y_col,
    x_label=None,
    y_label=None,
    title=None,
    quintile_col="income_quintile",
    y_max=100,
):

    sub = df[[x_col, y_col, quintile_col]].dropna(subset=[x_col, y_col])
    if sub.empty:
        print(f"No data for {x_col} vs {y_col}")
        return

    x_arr = sub[x_col].values.astype(float)
    y_arr = sub[y_col].values.astype(float)

    # OLS line
    slope, intercept = np.polyfit(x_arr, y_arr, 1)
    x_line = np.linspace(x_arr.min(), x_arr.max(), 300)
    y_line = intercept + slope * x_line

    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 4), dpi=300)

    # Scatter by quintile
    for q_label, color in QUINTILE_COLORS.items():
        mask = sub[quintile_col] == q_label
        if mask.any():
            ax.scatter(
                sub.loc[mask, x_col],
                sub.loc[mask, y_col],
                s=20,
                alpha=0.45,
                color=color,
                label=q_label
            )

    # OLS line
    ax.plot(
        x_line,
        y_line,
        linewidth=2,
        label=f"OLS (slope={slope:+.3f})"
    )
    ax.set_xlim(left=0)
    ax.set_title(title if title else f"{x_col} vs {y_col}")
    ax.set_xlabel(x_label if x_label else x_col)
    ax.set_ylabel(y_label if y_label else y_col)
    ax.set_ylim(0, y_max)

    ax.legend(title="Income Quintile", fontsize=8)
    plt.tight_layout()

```

# Data Sources
With the exception of food deserts, which are fixed to 2019, the scope of our data is from 2018 to 2024. Our data was sourced from sources across all levels of government. We used the Census’s 5 Year American Community Survey data to capture household income, demographics per tract. Finally, we used the USDA’s Economic Research Survey (ERS) to capture food desert data per census tract. We used this data as our independent variables. Our dependent variables came from the Illinois School Board of Education (ISBE) School Report Card. They were: chronic absenteeism, four-year graduation rate, English Language Arts (ELA) proficiency, and math proficiency. The ISBE defines chronic absenteeism as a student being absent for 10 or more percent of the school year, or 18 days. Both ELA and math proficiency are derived from standardized tests. We used a couple of different metrics constructed to model segregation that we encountered in the literature. These include the segregation quotient from Aguirre-Nuñez, Carlos, et al (2024) and the dissimilarity index Green (2022).

# Methodology
We wanted to use one of the machine learning methods from PPHA 30545 to help identify the most significant independent variables This was done both to demonstrate our command of the material and to mitigate the problem of overfitting. Given the amount of data, it’s likely that a standard OLS regression model would have been overfitted.

Given the criteria, our options were either a lasso regression, ridge regression, or an elastic net. We ultimately decided to use an elastic net because it incorporates both penalty terms present in the lasso and ridge regressions; the elastic net uses the L1 penalty term from lasso and the L2 penalty term from ridge. The formula for the elastic net is as follows: OLS + λ{α∥β∥1​+[(1−α​)/2] ∥β∥22} where α is the L1 formula from lasso, and 1-α is the L2 penalty term from ridge.

# Findings
To conform with the page requirement we will interpret the most interesting of the significant findings from each of our dependent variables. Our dependent variables are math test scores and 4 year graduation rate. 

We can see that math scores have a positive strong relationship with the number of households in a census tract.There is an adjusted R-squared of 0.64. This suggests that schools within denser neighborhoods have better math scores. This is likely the case since especially in Chicago, denser neighborhoods could be seen as more likely to live in. The graph below visualizes this relationship.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 6
#| fig-height: 4
#| dpi: 300
plot_scatter_with_ols(
    df=df,
    x_col="x_total_n_hh",
    y_col="y_math_prof",
    x_label="Total Households (Tract)",
    y_label="Post-Test Math Score",
    title="Impact of Household Density on Math Scores"
)

```

We can also see that the percentage of students on an IEP has a negative relationship with the graduation rate. There is an adjusted R squared of 0.87. This suggests that high schools with more students on an IEP experience lower graduation rates.The graph below visualizes this relationship. 
```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 6
#| fig-height: 4
#| dpi: 300
plot_scatter_with_ols(
    df=df,
    x_col="x_pct_iep",
    y_col="y_grad_4yr",
    x_label="% IEP Students",
    y_label="4 Year Graduation Rate",
    title="Impact of IEP on Graduation Rate"
)

```


For a more in depth look at our findings please look at our dashboard. The dashboard will allow you to predict the change in Y variables based on changes in our X variables. The link is: https://coffinatededu.streamlit.app/


# Assumptions and Discussion
As mentioned in the data section, all of our data is publicly available; unfortunately, that also means that we did not have access to some of the proprietary data needed to fill gaps in the publicly available data. This section will discuss the assumptions we made to address this problem.

Our food desert data has been fixed to 2019, the latest publicly available data from the 2019 ERS. The City of Chicago’s Open Data Portal has slightly more recent data, but does not encompass the entirety of Cook County. Given that mapping out food deserts across the county is not fully within the scope of this project, we decided to keep the 2019 status constant across the entire scope of our work. 

Another key assumption we made was the math and ELA scores are independent of each other.  Ünal et al. (2023) suggests a strong relationship between the two scores, (r = 0.52), contradicting our assumption. Because there were a limited number of dependent variables available, we chose to assume otherwise.

Education mobility was one of the metrics used as an independent variable, this is the ratio of the change in student population to the total student population. Because the scope of our research only included public high schools, we chose to assume that all attrition remained within the public school system. Ultimately, this is our justification for including student mobility as an independent variable.

Our final assumption is that the student population is representative of their census tract and vice versa. Throughout our project we used tract level data and school level data interchangeably. This assumption is our justification for doing so. 

# REFERENCES:
Aguirre-Nuñez, Carlos, et al. “Socio-Spatial Analysis of Migrant School Students According to Residential Segregation and Academic Performance in Santiago de Chile.” Heliyon 10, no. 9 (2024). 
https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11076905/

City of Chicago. CTA – “L” (Rail) Lines – Shapefile. 
Chicago Data Portal. \\
https://data.cityofchicago.org/Transportation/CTA-L-Rail-Lines-
Shapefile/53r7-y88m

Green, Rodney D. “The Dissimilarity Index: A Tutorial.” Howard University College of Arts and Sciences Centers. Accessed March 4, 2026. 
https://coascenters.howard.edu/dissimilarity-index-tutorial

Illinois State Board of Education. Illinois Report Card. 
Accessed March 4, 2026. \\
https://www.illinoisreportcard.com/.

Unal, Zehra & Greene, Nathaniel & Lin, Xin & Geary, David. (2023). 
What is the Source of the Correlation Between Reading and Mathematics Achievement? 
Two Meta-Analytic Studies. Educational Psychology Review. 35. 10.1007/s10648-023-09717-5. 

University of Chicago. Canvas Course Site, Course 66732. Canvas Learning 
Management System. https://canvas.uchicago.edu/courses/66732

United States Census Bureau. Table B17026: Ratio of Income to Poverty Level of Families. 2015–2019 American Community Survey 5-Year Estimates. 
https://data.census.gov/table/B17026

United States Census Bureau. Table B17026: Ratio of Income to Poverty Level of Families. 2020–2024 American Community Survey 5-Year Estimates.
 https://data.census.gov/table/ACSDT5Y2024.B17026

U.S. Department of Agriculture, Economic Research Service. Food Access Research Atlas. 
https://www.ers.usda.gov/data-products/food-access-research-atlas/
